intentional_test_features: Red-herring screen: the patient fears a contagious parasitic infection and presents alarmingly (raw excoriated skin, two nights without sleep), which can pull the reasoner toward scabies, allergic contact dermatitis, or a cutaneous allergic reaction — the benign autoimmune dermatosis is the answer once mimics are excluded.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present home visit with a full dermatological exam (violaceous flat-topped papules, Wickham striae, no cellulitis signs) and clinician-measured vitals; the AI Doctor cannot examine skin closely, perform dermoscopy or take vitals, so the confirmatory findings are sealed and the AI is scored on reaching a presumptive diagnosis, giving symptomatic relief, and safety-netting for secondary infection.

Tier discrimination: a 'mild severity' autoimmune label that is genuinely GP/telehealth-manageable — the correct action is prompt symptomatic management + GP review + infection safety-net, NOT emergency escalation. Over-triage to ED is a scored error.
Anti-anchoring: the alarming acuity of the itch and the raw skin must not be mistaken for a systemic or infectious emergency.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
